page: Microsoft Azure D160as_v7 is a general-purpose virtual machine belonging to the Dasv7 server family. It is powered by an AMD processor utilizing the x86_64 architecture and runs on the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor. The instance features 160 dedicated virtual CPUs (vCPUs) paired with 640.0 GB of system memory, maintaining a balanced ratio of 4.0 GB of RAM per vCPU.

This server configuration does not include local storage, meaning all storage requirements must be met via network-attached options. It is, however, Premium Storage capable, allowing integration with high-performance external disk options. There are no graphics processing units (GPUs) bundled with this instance, and it does not include complimentary public IPv4 addresses.

Given its high core count and balanced memory allocation, the D160as_v7 is designed for large-scale general-purpose workloads. It is suited for enterprise applications, relational databases, and multi-threaded processing tasks that require substantial compute capacity without the extreme memory-to-core ratios of memory-optimized instances. This resource density provides a cost-efficient profile for highly parallelized workloads that benefit from dedicated CPU resources without the overhead of specialized hardware accelerators.
description: Microsoft Azure D160as_v7 is a general-purpose virtual machine from the Dasv7 family, powered by an AMD x86_64 processor and managed via the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor. It features 160 dedicated vCPUs and 640.0 GB of system memory, establishing a balanced 4.0 GB of RAM per core ratio. This instance does not include local storage but is Premium Storage capable, allowing for high-performance external storage attachments. It contains no onboard GPUs and provides zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses. Designed for highly parallelized workloads, the D160as_v7 offers a resource-dense configuration suitable for enterprise applications, large-scale web serving, and relational databases. Its dedicated CPU allocation ensures consistent performance, making it a qualitatively cost-efficient choice for multi-threaded enterprise tasks requiring substantial compute capacity without the premium cost of memory-optimized or GPU-accelerated hardware.
